nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on shallow reef and rubble bottoms — coral fragments, loose rubble piles, rocky overhangs, and seaweed-covered hard substrates — typically at depths of roughly 3–15 m, though has been recorded much deeper on hard bottoms and seamounts. Tends to remain cryptic among rockwork by day.

diet.notes: Primarily an opportunistic algae grazer and scavenger. Has been documented eating bubble algae (Valonia, Ventricaria), hair algae, Dictyota, some Caulerpa species, turf algae, and other complex macroalgae, with blunt scraping claws well suited to harvesting algae from live rock. Supplemental meaty foods (sinking pellets, mysis, finely chopped marine fish/shellfish) are commonly recommended once nuisance algae populations in the tank are reduced, as underfed adults have been known to begin sampling coral polyps and sessile invertebrates.

avoidWith: predatory fish that target small crabs (large wrasses, hawkfish, triggerfish, lionfish)
larger aggressive crabs (including adult Emerald Crabs in small systems)
delicate LPS and softie corals once the crab is well grown and underfed
any copper-based medication in the system

summary: The Green Clinging Crab (Mithraculus forceps) is a small Caribbean reef crab closely related to the Emerald Crab, valued for its ability to graze bubble algae, hair algae, and macroalgae from live rock. It is generally considered reef-safe with caution.
careNotes:
The Green Clinging Crab is a small Western Atlantic and Caribbean clinging crab in the same genus as the popular Emerald Crab (Mithraculus sculptus). Despite the common name under which it is occasionally sold, this species (Mithraculus forceps) is the smaller, red-to-ruby-bodied relative of the emerald-green M. sculptus — in the trade it also appears under the names Mithrax Clinging Crab, Red Mithrax, Ruby Mithrax, Ruby Clinging Crab, and Red-ridged Clinging Crab. The colour labelling in the trade is inconsistent and has caused recurring confusion between the two species. Adults reach a carapace (shell) width of roughly 1–2.5 cm, noticeably smaller than M. sculptus.

In the wild it occurs across Bermuda, the southeastern US coast, Florida, the Gulf of Mexico, the Bahamas, the Caribbean Sea, and south to Brazil, generally on shallow reef and rubble bottoms between about 3 and 15 m (though hard-bottom records extend much deeper). In the aquarium it tends to spend daylight hours tucked into caves, rubble piles, and shaded overhangs in the rockwork, emerging to graze on rock surfaces and scavenge. A mature reef aquascape with ample live rock, rubble, and hiding places is generally advised; a 75 L (~20 gal) nano-reef is a reasonable practical floor despite some retailer pages quoting 5 gal — the smaller figure tends to assume juveniles and does not account for grazing range.

Like all decapod crustaceans, it is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to swings in salinity, pH, or temperature. Drip-acclimation over 1–2 hours is generally advised on introduction. Stable reef-tank parameters, good nutrition, and occasional iodine supplementation are commonly cited as supporting clean molts; freshly molted crabs are vulnerable for 24–48 hours and tend to hide. The shed exuvia (the discarded shell) should be left in the tank — the crab will consume it and recover calcium and trace elements in the process.

Reef safety: the species is generally considered reef-safe with caution. Has been documented as one of the more reliable bubble-algae predators in the trade (similar to its Emerald congener) and tends to be less aggressive than M. sculptus, but well-grown adults have been known to sample LPS polyps, zoanthids, and other sessile invertebrates if underfed. Supplemental meaty feeding once nuisance algae is reduced is commonly advised. The species is also kept successfully in sumps and refugiums as an algae-control utility crab where reef-tank coral nipping is a concern. Generally kept singly per system; multiple individuals tend to spar over territory in smaller tanks.

There is genuine confusion in the hobby press over which Mithraculus species the 'Green Clinging Crab' label refers to. The two are sympatric Caribbean species in the same family (Mithracidae) and are routinely confused.
breedingNotes: Mithraculus forceps has been the subject of pilot aquaculture studies in which planktonic larvae are reared in dedicated systems with live rotifer and Artemia feeds, but routine captive larval rearing is not practical for a typical home reef. Egg-carrying females do occasionally appear in well-fed display tanks; the larvae are not expected to survive standard reef filtration. The species is not realistically breedable at home aquarium scale.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria does not maintain a dedicated product page for Mithraculus forceps; only the Emerald Crab (Mithraculus sculptus) page is available. Used the Emerald Crab page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/product/628/?pcatid=628) as the genus-level baseline for water parameters (22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), reef-safe status, and copper sensitivity, then cross-checked species-specific traits via dedicated M. forceps sources below.

additional.name: Tampa Bay Saltwater — Ruby Clinging Crab (Mithraculus forceps)
additional.notes: Designated Reef2Reef Wiki secondary returned HTTP 403; substituted Tampa Bay Saltwater as the first responding species-specific secondary source. Used for reef-safe-with-caution status, diet (macroalgae similar to Emerald Crab, may sample coral polyps when full grown and underfed), refugium/sump compatibility, and congener relationship.

additional.name: Reef Cleaners — Ruby Mithrax Crab
additional.notes: Used for max carapace size (~25 mm / 2.5 cm), specific algae targets (bubble algae, hair algae, Dictyota, some Caulerpa, turf algae), Caribbean origin, and reef-safe-with-caution status.

additional.name: Salty Underground — Algae Eating Ruby Red Crab (Mithraculus forceps)
additional.notes: Used for care level (beginner), reef-safe rating (peaceful, omnivore), and additional water-parameter cross-check (22–28°C, pH 8.0–8.3). Quoted 5 gal minimum tank size which was rejected per the playbook's cautious tank-size rule (rounded up to 75 L).

additional.name: ReefGuide — Red-Ridged Clinging Crab (Caribbean)
additional.notes: Used for adult size (1–2.5 cm), depth range (3–15 m typical reef occurrence), full geographic range (Caribbean, Bahamas, Florida, Bermuda, Gulf of Mexico), and family-level taxonomy (Mithracidae, Decapoda).

additional.name: WoRMS / GulfBase — Mithraculus forceps taxonomic record
additional.notes: Used for canonical taxonomy (Arthropoda > Malacostraca > Decapoda > Mithracidae > Mithraculus) and synonym list (Mithrax forceps, Mithraculus hirsutipes). GulfBase (https://www.gulfbase.org/species/mithraculus-forceps) cross-checked the extended depth range (0–90 m on hard bottoms) and the broader Bermuda → Brazil distribution.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts roughly every 3–6 weeks under stable reef conditions; younger and faster-growing individuals molt more frequently. Freshly molted crabs are soft and vulnerable for about 24–48 hours and tend to hide; leave the shed exuvia in the tank for the crab to consume (recovers calcium and trace elements). Stable salinity, calcium, and trace-element supplementation (iodine in particular) are commonly cited as supporting clean molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Planktonic larvae do not survive standard reef filtration; documented aquaculture work exists at research scale but is not practical at hobby scale.
